question: Identify the root cause of the network fault: which device, what changed, and why it breaks.
description:
R2 sets the IS-IS overload bit (`set-overload-bit on-startup 300`,
but applied without the on-startup qualifier for live effect). Every
other router sees R2's LSP with the OL bit set and treats R2 as
non-transit — they will *not* compute paths *through* R2 to reach any
other node. R0 is only connected to R2 → R0 becomes isolated from
R3/R4/R5/R6/R7. Heavy data-plane impact.
expected_rca:
Root cause: on 100.64.0.102 (R2), the IS-IS overload bit was set (set-overload-bit).
R2 advertises its LSP with the OL bit, which by IS-IS SPF rules signals every other router to exclude R2 as a transit node, so no node computes a path through R2. R0 is single-homed to R2, so R0 cannot reach R3/R4/R5/R6/R7 and is isolated from the rest of the topology.
Fix: clear the overload bit (no set-overload-bit) on R2.
expected_symptoms_summary:
No fault syslog observed after apply — silent scenario; IS-IS
overload-bit propagation is a quiet LSP attribute change.
Diagnostic ground truth lives in expected_state_diffs.
expected_state_diffs: R2 `show isis database verbose | inc Overload`: OL=1 (baseline: OL=0). On every other router, `show isis database R2` shows the same OL bit set in R2's LSP.
R2 `show running-config router isis LAB`: `set-overload-bit` line present (baseline: absent).
Any router `show isis topology`: paths through R2 omitted — R2 is no longer a transit node.
R0 `show route 192.0.2.6` (or any non-R2 loopback): Network not in table — R0 is the only router solely connected to R2 and is now isolated.
R5 `show route 192.0.2.10` (R0's loopback): Network not in table — R5 can't reach R0 because the only path traverses R2 which is non-transit.

apply.commands:
configure terminal
router isis LAB
 set-overload-bit

revert.commands:
configure terminal
router isis LAB
 no set-overload-bit
